item.title: Europe’s Growing Jet Fuel Problem Sends Airlines Into a Spin
item.description: The continent has lost about 20% of its usual supplies due to the war in Iran.

item.title: Europe’s Attempt to Protect Children Online Crashes into Its Privacy Laws
item.description: The region tries to balance the rights of users online with keeping children safe

item.title: Oil-Price Surge Fails to Stoke US Energy-Sector Enthusiasm
item.description: High crude prices are yet sparking enthusiasm among Texas oil producers.

item.title: Investors Bet on Trade Schools as AI Clouds White-Collar Job Market
item.description: Growing concerns about a white-collar job apocalypse are helping to turn once-sleepy trade school stocks into investor darlings.

item.title: Richer European Countries Question Multi-Trillion Budget Talks
item.description: Tensions over the European Union’s next long-term budget surfaced in negotiations among member states, with the bloc’s richer members insisting that their contributions to the common budget be reduced.

item.title: Europe’s Aerospace Stocks Rack Up Biggest Weekly Losses in Years
item.description: European aerospace stocks are suffering some of their biggest weekly losses in years on concerns that fuel prices will stay high due to the Iran war, curbing air travel demand and eroding profits.

item.title: Russia Cuts Key Rate With Eye on Iran War and Budget Risks
item.description: Russia’s central bank continued lowering borrowing costs, delivering some relief to an economy that’s losing steam while treading cautiously due to uncertainty around the budget and the war in the Middle East.

item.title: The Hottest Phone for Kids Right Now Is a $100 Landline
item.description: The Tin Can has gone viral over the past year, mostly through word of mouth. Now schools want to get even more of them in students' homes.

item.title: Tech Bulls Are Taking Charge of the Stock Market
item.description: Nasdaq 100 is leading the rebound from the March lows.

item.title: Petro Speeds $7 Billion Pension Transfers, Risking Market Shock
item.description: Colombia’s government is pressing ahead with a plan to transfer about $7 billion from private pension savings into the public retirement system, a move that risks destabilizing local markets as funds may be forced to rapidly liquidate assets to comply.

item.title: War Squeezes Global Mining as Diesel and Acid Supplies Tighten
item.description: From the Australian outback to Ethiopia and the Democratic Republic of Congo, the global mining industry is beginning to feel the effects of disruption caused by the war in Iran.

item.title: Trump Suggests He’d Be Open to Alternative Probe of Fed’s Powell
item.description: President Donald Trump said that he’d be open to alternative ways to investigate Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, potentially offering an off-ramp to a current legal battle that is holding up the confirmation of the president’s new pick to lead the central bank, according to news outlet Semafor.

item.title: Kazimir Says Slight ECB Rate Increase May Be Necessary on War
item.description: European Central Bank Governing Council member Peter Kazimir suggested the Iran war might necessitate an eventual interest-rate hike.

item.title: Data Centers Are Finding a Surprising Way to Deploy Batteries
item.description: By pairing energy storage with natural gas, hyperscalers are able to get power faster and do so behind the meter.

item.title: Revolut Winds Down Commodities Service in Some European Markets
item.description: Revolut Ltd. is shuttering its commodities business for trading precious metals in parts of Europe.

item.title: Bank of England Surveys Suggest Energy Shock Isn’t Fueling Wages
item.description: UK firms are planning their largest price hikes in over two years but the energy price shock from the Iran war has yet to stir wage demands as anxiety mounts among consumers and businesses, Bank of England surveys showed.

item.title: MS NOW Looks to Be a More Social Network With New App
item.description: MS NOW, the media network formerly called MSNBC, is using Saturday’s White House Correspondents’ Dinner as an opportunity to debut its new rebrand in Washington.The network is hosting a brunch for student journalists and on-air talent on Friday. Then, following the Saturday dinner, MS NOW has invited Washington insiders to what’s being billed as a “subversive” underground after party at a former trolley car station nearby.

item.title: Economists Raise US Inflation Forecasts as Iran War Drags On
item.description: Economists raised their estimates for US inflation and said they expect only one Federal Reserve interest-rate cut this year amid elevated energy costs sparked by the Iran war.

item.title: SLB Seen Acquiring S&P Global’s Subsurface Oil Software Business
item.description: SLB, the world’s largest oilfield service provider, is expected to announce Friday plans to acquire S&P Global Energy Inc.’s geoscience and petroleum-engineering software business, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Altman Versus Musk: How the Biggest Feud In Tech Landed in Court
item.description: The OpenAI CEO fell out with the world’s richest person years ago. Now Musk is trying to thwart Altman’s plans for the leading AI platform.

item.title: China Seeks Closer Thai, Cambodia Ties While Warning on Scams
item.description: China’s top envoy Wang Yi signaled Beijing’s concerns about online scams emanating from Southeast Asia as he visited Thailand and Cambodia in a bid to boost links with both nations.

item.title: ECB Moves to Smooth Digital Euro Rollout With Standards Deals
item.description: The European Central Bank moved to minimize adoption costs and encourage early coordination among those involved in its digital euro project, part of efforts to get the currency up and running this decade.

item.title: Repsol, BP Accused of Market Abuse by Spanish Fuel Firm Rival
item.description: Repsol SA, BP Plc and slate of other oil firms have been accused of abusing market dominance and anti-competitive practices in a complaint filed by a Spanish wholesaler of refined fuels.

item.title: Global Bonds Set for Worst Week in a Month as US-Iran Risks Rise
item.description: Global bond markets are heading for their worst week in a month as investors grow increasingly uneasy about a stalemate between the US and Iran.

item.title: China to Curb US Investment in Tech Companies After Meta Deal
item.description: Chinese regulators plan to restrict technology firms including some of the country’s highest-profile AI pioneers from accepting US capital without government approval, part of Beijing’s broader response to Meta Platforms Inc.’s controversial acquisition of startup Manus.

item.title: China Allows Global Funds to Trade Government Bond Futures
item.description: China is allowing foreign investors to participate in government bond futures trading starting Friday, in a landmark step toward opening up its debt market to global capital.

item.title: Yara Boosts Profit as Iran War Sends Fertilizer Prices Soaring
item.description: Fertilizer giant Yara International ASA posted higher-than-expected first quarter earnings as the Iran war effectively halted transit through the Strait of Hormuz, hurting global trade of crop nutrients and pushing up prices.

item.title: Specialist Engineering Firm TSK Plans €150 Million IPO in Spain
item.description: TSK Electronica y Electricidad SA is planning an initial public offering in what would be the first sizeable main market listing in Spain this year.

item.title: India’s Shapoorji Gets Nod to Delay Bond Days Before Maturity
item.description: Indian infrastructure conglomerate Shapoorji Pallonji Group has secured creditor approval to delay payment on one of the nation’s biggest high-yield bonds, just days ahead of the April 30 maturity, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: China Imposes Export Curbs on EU Firms Over Taiwan Arms Sales
item.description: China added seven European entities to its export control list, citing national security interests and their involvement in arms sales to Taiwan.
